title: “Monks, these five perceptions, when developed and cultivated, are of great fruit, great benefit, merging with the deathless, ending in the deathless. What five? The…
content: “Monks, these five perceptions, when developed and cultivated, are of great fruit, great benefit, merging with the deathless, ending in the deathless. What five? The perception of foulness, the perception of death, the perception of peril, the perception of disgust with food, the perception of non-delight in the entire world. These five perceptions, monks, when developed and cultivated, are of great fruit, great benefit, merging with the deathless, ending in the deathless.”

title: “And, monks, shrouded by ignorance and bound by craving, this body of the wise person is generated. That ignorance the wise person abandons. That craving…
content: “And, monks, shrouded by ignorance and bound by craving, this body of the wise person is generated. That ignorance the wise person abandons. That craving the wise person destroys. Why is that? Monks, the wise person lives the holy life for the complete destruction of suffering. Therefore, with the breakup of the body, after death, the wise person does not go to another body. Not going to another body, he is freed from birth, aging, death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, grief, and despair. I say that he is freed from suffering.”

title: “For the most part, monks, beings find pleasure in sensual pleasures. But a clansman who has set aside the sickle and the carrying-pole, gone forth…
content: “For the most part, monks, beings find pleasure in sensual pleasures. But a clansman who has set aside the sickle and the carrying-pole, gone forth from the household life into homelessness, is justifiably called ‘a clansman who has gone forth out of faith.’ Why is that? Sensual pleasures are found with youth, these sensual pleasures or others. And, monks, low sensual pleasures, middling sensual pleasures, and supreme sensual pleasures — all are classed as sensual pleasures.”

title: “Sunakkhatta, suppose there is a very poisonous viper. Then a man comes who desires to live, not to die, who desires happiness and loathes suffering.…
content:
“Sunakkhatta, suppose there is a very poisonous viper. Then a man comes who desires to live, not to die, who desires happiness and loathes suffering. What do you think, Sunakkhatta? Would that man give his hand or his foot to that viper if he knew: ‘If I am bitten by this snake, I will meet death or deadly suffering’?”
“No, venerable sir.”
“So too, Sunakkhatta, a monk should surely guard himself in regard to the six sense-bases. Knowing that ‘Attachment is the root of suffering,’ he becomes unattached and liberated by the destruction of attachment. It does not happen that he would still focus his body on attachment or direct his mind to attachment.”

title: Elder Māṇava: “I saw an old person, I saw a sick person in pain, I saw a dead person, With life exhausted — Therefore I…
content:
Elder Māṇava:
“I saw an old person,
I saw a sick person in pain,
I saw a dead person,
With life exhausted —
Therefore I went forth,
Leaving the home life,
Abandoning sensual pleasures
That are enticing and agreeable.”

title: “Thus, great king, a monk contemplates the five hindrances not yet abandoned as a debt, as a disease, as a prison, as slavery, as a…
content: “Thus, great king, a monk contemplates the five hindrances not yet abandoned as a debt, as a disease, as a prison, as slavery, as a desert road. And, great king, just as being without debt, without disease, freed from prison, freed from slavery, in a safe and pleasant land — so, great king, a monk contemplates the five hindrances when they have been abandoned.”

title: “Good sirs, if a monk, reflecting, knows: ‘I dwell much with a greedy mind; I dwell much with a hateful mind; I dwell much with…
content: “Good sirs, if a monk, reflecting, knows: ‘I dwell much with a greedy mind; I dwell much with a hateful mind; I dwell much with a mind overcome by sloth and torpor; I dwell much with restlessness; I dwell much with doubt; I dwell much with anger; I dwell much with a defiled mind; I dwell much with bodily fever; I dwell much with laziness; I dwell much with an unconcentrated mind’ — then that monk should make a strong wish, arouse effort, be energetic, strive, be strong-minded, not fall away, be mindful and clearly comprehending for the abandonment of those evil, unwholesome states. Just as, good sirs, if a turban or head were on fire, one would make a strong wish, arouse effort, be energetic, strive, be strong-minded, not fall away, be mindful and clearly comprehending to extinguish that turban or head — so too, that monk should make a strong wish … for the abandonment of those evil, unwholesome states. If a monk, reflecting, knows: ‘I dwell much with a mind free from greed; I dwell much with a mind free from hatred; I dwell much with a mind free from sloth and torpor; I dwell much with a mind free from restlessness; I dwell much with a mind that has overcome doubt; I dwell much with a mind free from anger; I dwell much with an undefiled mind; I dwell much with a body free from fever; I dwell much with an energetic, striving mind; I dwell much with a concentrated mind’ — then that monk, established in those wholesome states, should further devote himself to the destruction of the defilements.”

title: Like a newly decorated medicine box, This foul body is adorned for the occasion — Enough to fool a fool, But not one who seeks…
content:
Like a newly decorated medicine box,
This foul body is adorned for the occasion —
Enough to fool a fool,
But not one who seeks the far shore.

The hunter set his trap and snare,
The wise deer does not approach,
Having eaten the crops, we go our way,
Leaving the trapper to lament.

title: Venerable Udaya: “I have come with a question — To the meditator, sitting dustless, Duty done, without defilements, Who has reached the far shore —…
content:
Venerable Udaya:
“I have come with a question —
To the meditator, sitting dustless,
Duty done, without defilements,
Who has reached the far shore —
Speak the knowledge of liberation,
The breaking of ignorance.”

The Blessed One replied to Udaya:
“Abandon desire for sensual pleasures,
Both sorrow and grief;
Abandon sloth and torpor,
And prevent remorse.

Knowledge of liberation, I say,
Is the breaking of ignorance —
Purified by equanimity and mindfulness,
With reflection leading the way.”

Venerable Udaya:
“What binds the world?
What is its range?
By abandoning what is Nibbāna called?”

The Blessed One:
“The world is bound by delight;
Directed thought is its range.
By abandoning craving,
Nibbāna is called.”

title: “Monks, here a monk, when there is sensual desire in him, knows: ‘There is sensual desire in me’; or when there is no sensual desire…
content: “Monks, here a monk, when there is sensual desire in him, knows: ‘There is sensual desire in me’; or when there is no sensual desire in him, knows: ‘There is no sensual desire in me.’ And he knows how unarisen sensual desire arises; he knows how arisen sensual desire is abandoned; and he knows how abandoned sensual desire does not arise in the future.”
